angle: What it's like building a product while working full-time
threads: Building kakisewa while working full-time is basically: 8am-6pm doing my actual job, then 9pm-midnight arguing with myself about database schemas. Currently cold emailing 8,471 Malaysian rental agents at 100/day. Did I have time to build this? No. Did I do it anyway? Unfortunately yes.

slides: What building a product while working full-time actually looks like
Your 'deep work' hours are 10pm on a Tuesday
You ship features in 90-minute windows between meetings
Every weekend is a negotiation between rest and roadmap
Currently cold emailing 8,471 rental agents. 100 a day. One by one.
It's not romantic. It's just stubbornness with a Notion doc.
But it's the only way I know how to find out if this is real
carousel.caption: kakisewa is getting built in the gaps. Sharing the whole thing as it happens.

angle: Day 1 — Building in public: zero paying customers
caption:
I built a SaaS and have zero paying customers.

Here is what week 1 actually looked like.

What are you building right now? Drop it in the comments.
threads_text:
I built a SaaS and have zero paying customers.

Here is what week 1 actually looked like: the launch nobody saw, the Stripe dashboard I kept refreshing, and what building while employed actually costs you.

What are you building right now?

slides.main_text: I built a SaaS and have **zero paying customers.**
slides.sub_text: Here is what week 1 actually looked like.
After 7 days live. Fully launched. Real people able to sign up.
slides.bubble: I kept refreshing Stripe expecting something to happen. Nothing happened.

slides.main_text: I built it in **evenings and weekends.**
slides.sub_text: While working full-time at a US startup in Malaysia.
slides.sub_text_2: No co-founder. No investors. No announcement.
slides.bubble: My colleagues think I go to bed early.

slides.sub_text: No Product Hunt. No X post. No fanfare.
slides.sub_text_2: Just pushed it live and waited to see if anyone noticed.
slides.bubble: The scariest part was not launching. It was nobody noticing.

slides.main_text: The gap between **it is ready** and anyone caring.
slides.sub_text: Nobody is waiting for your product. You have to go find them.
slides.bubble: Week 1 taught me: building is the easy part. Distribution is the job.

slides.main_text: Zero customers does not mean **zero progress.**
slides.sub_text: It means you are still early enough to get it right.
slides.follow_cta: Follow to know what happens next or save this for later.
slides.bubble: Month 2 starts tomorrow. Let's see if the emails start converting.

angle: What working remotely for an overseas startup is actually like
caption: Everyone romanticises working remotely for an overseas startup. Here is what it actually looks like.
threads_text: Working remotely for an overseas startup sounds cool until you are on a late-night Zoom while your friends are out. The pay is decent. The timezone is not. You are always half a day behind everyone who matters. Still beats a lot of alternatives though.

slides.main_text: Working remotely for an **overseas startup** sounds like the dream.
slides.sub_text: Here is what nobody tells you about it.

slides.title: The time tax
slides.sub_text: Hours between me and the rest of the team, every single day.
slides.bubble: My most productive hours are when my boss is asleep. Make of that what you will.

slides.title: The schedule
slides.main_text: Your **mornings are free.** Your evenings are not.
slides.sub_text: All the real meetings happen at the end of your day, so you learn to protect your mornings like they are the only hours you actually own.
slides.bubble: I have perfected the art of looking alert on camera late at night.

slides.title: The math
slides.main_text: The **pay gap** is real and it works in your favour.
slides.sub_text: Headquarters salary, local cost of living. The math is genuinely good, and this is the one part that lives up to the hype.
slides.bubble: Not going to pretend this is not a big part of why most people do it.

slides.title: The downside
slides.main_text: But you are always **half a day behind** everyone who matters.
slides.sub_text: Decisions get made while you sleep, and you catch up in the morning to find the landscape has already shifted.
slides.bubble: Nothing like waking up to 47 Slack messages and a decision that affects your whole week.

slides.title: The opportunity
slides.main_text: So I started building in the **pockets of time I actually own.**
slides.sub_text: 8,471 cold emails out to potential customers. 100 a day, automated. Zero paying customers so far. But the mornings are mine.
slides.bubble: Running a side project while the day job sleeps. Very normal behaviour.

slides.title: The takeaway
slides.main_text: The **timezone gap** is the tax. The free mornings are the opportunity.
slides.sub_text: Still figuring out if I am playing it right.
slides.bubble: Ask me again once the side project actually makes money.

angle: I picked the most unsexy market possible.
caption:
Day 2. Still zero customers. But I picked this market on purpose.

Most people said it was not exciting enough to build for. I thought: good, fewer people will try to compete.

Swipe to see why I chose boring over cool, and whether it is actually smart or just cope.

Unsexy market or cool idea, which would you build? Vote in comments: U or C
threads_text:
Day 2 of building in public. I picked property agents as my market. They still track contracts in WhatsApp. Nobody thinks it is exciting. I think that is the point.

Poll: which would you build?
U = Unsexy market with real pain
C = Cool idea with hype

slides.main_text: I picked the most **unsexy market** possible.
slides.sub_text: Property agents who still track contracts in WhatsApp. And I think that is the point.

slides.title: The market
slides.sub_text: Property agents with no digital contract system, tracking everything in WhatsApp and sticky notes.
slides.stat_label: agents found. 100 cold emails going out every day.
slides.bubble: I have their emails. I do not have their money. Yet.

slides.title: The gap
slides.sub_text: Which either means it is a wide open opportunity, or everyone already tried and left. I am choosing to believe the first one.
slides.bubble: This is the part where I find out which.

slides.title: The validation
slides.main_text: The problem is **obvious once you talk to one agent.**
slides.sub_text: Contracts in WhatsApp, renewals tracked in their head, and nobody built a tool because nobody thought it was worth building.
slides.bubble: First agent I talked to just uses notes on their phone. That was my product validation.

slides.title: The advantage
slides.main_text: **Unsexy markets have real paying customers** and fewer competitors.
slides.sub_text: The pain is specific, the buyers exist, and nobody is pitching them at TechCrunch.
slides.bubble: My friends thought I was building something boring. I think boring pays rent.

slides.title: The pitch
slides.main_text: I chose this over a **'cool' idea** because the pain is specific.
slides.sub_text: Specific pain means the customer already knows they have a problem before you explain it, which makes the sales conversation much easier.
slides.bubble: Cool ideas need you to convince people they have a problem. Hard pass.

slides.title: The takeaway
slides.sub_text: Still zero paying customers, but I know exactly who I am building for.
slides.bubble: Unsexy market or cool idea, which would you build? Drop U or C in the comments.
